content:
First, note that “\(n^2\) has the same remainder upon division by 7, 11, 13” means
\[
n^2\equiv r\pmod7,\quad n^2\equiv r\pmod{11},\quad n^2\equiv r\pmod{13}
\]
for some \(r\in\{0,1,\dots,6\}\) (because the remainder mod 7 must lie in \(0,\dots,6\)).

1.  Since \(\gcd(7,11,13)=1\), we combine into
   \[
     n^2\equiv r\pmod{1001},\quad r\in\{0,1,2,3,4,5,6\}\,. 
   \]
2.  For each prime \(p\in\{7,11,13\}\), count solutions of 
   \[
     n^2\equiv r\pmod p.
   \]
   –  Mod 7 the quadratic residues are \(\{0,1,2,4\}\).  
   –  Mod 11 they are \(\{0,1,3,4,5,9\}\).  
   –  Mod 13 they are \(\{0,1,3,4,9,10,12\}\).  
   Intersecting these three sets (and remembering \(r\le6\)) leaves only  
   \[
     r=0,\;1,\;4.
   \]
3.  For each choice of \(r\in\{0,1,4\}\), the number of solutions mod 1001 is the product
   \[
   \bigl(\#\text{solutions mod7}\bigr)\times\bigl(\#\text{solutions mod11}\bigr)
   \times\bigl(\#\text{solutions mod13}\bigr).
   \]
   Checking case by case:
   \[
     r=0:\;(1\times1\times1)=1
     ,\qquad
     r=1:\;(2\times2\times2)=8
     ,\qquad
     r=4:\;(2\times2\times2)=8.
   \]
   So in total there are \(1+8+8=17\) residue‐classes mod 1001 that work.
4.  We want \(1\le n<2018\).  Since \(2\cdot1001=2002\), each residue‐class mod 1001 appears
   exactly twice among \(1,2,\dots,2002\), and among \(2003,\dots,2017\)
   it appears a third time exactly if its representative lies in \(\{1,2,\dots,15\}\).
   
   Checking small \(k=1,2,\dots,15\) by hand for the triple‐remainder condition shows only
   \(k=1\) and \(k=2\) work.  Hence exactly 2 of our 17 classes appear 3‐times, the other 15
   classes appear only 2‐times in the range \(1\le n<2018\).
5.  Total count
   \[
     2\cdot3 \;+\;15\cdot2 \;=\;6+30 \;=\;36.
   \]

Answer: \(\boxed{36}\).

message.content:
First, note that “\(n^2\) has the same remainder upon division by 7, 11, 13” means
\[
n^2\equiv r\pmod7,\quad n^2\equiv r\pmod{11},\quad n^2\equiv r\pmod{13}
\]
for some \(r\in\{0,1,\dots,6\}\) (because the remainder mod 7 must lie in \(0,\dots,6\)).

1.  Since \(\gcd(7,11,13)=1\), we combine into
   \[
     n^2\equiv r\pmod{1001},\quad r\in\{0,1,2,3,4,5,6\}\,. 
   \]
2.  For each prime \(p\in\{7,11,13\}\), count solutions of 
   \[
     n^2\equiv r\pmod p.
   \]
   –  Mod 7 the quadratic residues are \(\{0,1,2,4\}\).  
   –  Mod 11 they are \(\{0,1,3,4,5,9\}\).  
   –  Mod 13 they are \(\{0,1,3,4,9,10,12\}\).  
   Intersecting these three sets (and remembering \(r\le6\)) leaves only  
   \[
     r=0,\;1,\;4.
   \]
3.  For each choice of \(r\in\{0,1,4\}\), the number of solutions mod 1001 is the product
   \[
   \bigl(\#\text{solutions mod7}\bigr)\times\bigl(\#\text{solutions mod11}\bigr)
   \times\bigl(\#\text{solutions mod13}\bigr).
   \]
   Checking case by case:
   \[
     r=0:\;(1\times1\times1)=1
     ,\qquad
     r=1:\;(2\times2\times2)=8
     ,\qquad
     r=4:\;(2\times2\times2)=8.
   \]
   So in total there are \(1+8+8=17\) residue‐classes mod 1001 that work.
4.  We want \(1\le n<2018\).  Since \(2\cdot1001=2002\), each residue‐class mod 1001 appears
   exactly twice among \(1,2,\dots,2002\), and among \(2003,\dots,2017\)
   it appears a third time exactly if its representative lies in \(\{1,2,\dots,15\}\).
   
   Checking small \(k=1,2,\dots,15\) by hand for the triple‐remainder condition shows only
   \(k=1\) and \(k=2\) work.  Hence exactly 2 of our 17 classes appear 3‐times, the other 15
   classes appear only 2‐times in the range \(1\le n<2018\).
5.  Total count
   \[
     2\cdot3 \;+\;15\cdot2 \;=\;6+30 \;=\;36.
   \]

Answer: \(\boxed{36}\).
